judge_rationale: Complete, correct recursive BST implementation that handles all operations (insert, search, delete with all three cases), edge cases (duplicates, empty tree), and meets all test requirements with clear documentation and thoughtful design explanations.

Exceptional submission: all methods correctly implemented with proper handling of duplicates, empty tree deletion, and all three deletion cases; clean recursive design; comprehensive docstring; passes all test requirements.

Complete and correct implementation with all methods working properly, comprehensive handling of all edge cases including duplicates and empty tree operations, clear and well-documented code with thoughtful recursive design.

Exceptional solution: functionally correct for all test cases, demonstrates deep understanding of lazy initialization for mocking compatibility, proper thread-safe locking, continuous token refill calculation, and capacity enforcement. Code is publication-quality with clear insights explaining design choices.

Complete, correct implementation that passes all test cases. Thread-safe with proper lock usage, lazy initialization elegantly handles mocking constraints, and continuous refill logic is accurate. Explanation and trace-through validate the approach thoroughly.
Correct, complete implementation with proper thread-safety and continuous refill; minor code duplication between allow() and available_tokens() methods is the only gap.

Exceptional submission: all 3 bugs correctly identified, explained, and fixed with proper shared-lock pattern; code is formatted correctly with clear inline comments; solution would pass all test cases including concurrent access scenarios; minor room for improvement in discussing performance trade-offs (pop(0) vs deque) is beyond task scope.

Exceptional. All three bugs correctly identified, explained, and fixed. Condition variables now properly share the lock, notify() calls are safe, and get() correctly removes items with pop(0). Code is well-commented, properly formatted, and includes valuable insights about threading semantics.

All three bugs correctly identified and fixed with sound reasoning; code properly formatted with inline comments; explains root causes and includes educational insight on thread synchronization; solution will pass all test cases.

Complete and correct implementation achieving O(1) for both get and put operations using the required doubly-linked list and hashmap. All test cases pass; code is production-quality with effective use of dummy nodes to eliminate edge cases and clear helper methods.

Correct, complete O(1) implementation using sentinel nodes and hashmap. All tests pass, code is clean and idiomatic, with clear explanation and appropriate educational insight. Minor: could explicitly discuss space complexity or alternative trade-offs.

Exceptional solution: correct O(1) implementation with all test cases passing, complete handling of all requirements including edge cases, clean modular code with descriptive helpers, and clear explanation of the doubly-linked list + hash map design pattern.

Exceptional solution: correctly implements all Markdown elements, properly orders inline formatting (bold before italic), handles paragraph grouping and blank-line separation, prevents heading wrapping in

tags, and should pass all provided tests.
Solution correctly implements all markdown features with proper greedy matching (links→bold→italic), handles edge cases gracefully, and is well-structured and readable.

Correct implementation of all required Markdown features with proper block grouping, greedy matching, and inline element processing; code should pass all test cases; excellent explanation and clean structure with minor gaps in handling complex edge cases not specified in requirements.

Complete, correct implementation with both sorting algorithms properly benchmarked and all test cases passing; three-way partition optimization for duplicates shows good algorithmic understanding; code is clear and well-organized with helpful educational insights.

Exceptional submission: both sorting algorithms are correctly implemented with proper benchmarking logic, all edge cases are handled correctly, code is clear and well-documented, and the three-way partitioning design choice demonstrates sophisticated understanding of algorithm trade-offs.

Correct implementations of both sorting algorithms with proper benchmarking strategy, handles all edge cases and test requirements, 3-way quicksort partition choice is robust and well-justified, code is clear and well-structured.

Excellent state machine implementation that correctly handles all test cases including quoted fields, escaped quotes, embedded newlines, trailing newlines, empty fields, and custom delimiters. Code is clear with insightful explanations; the trailing newline logic is particularly clever. Minor limitation: doesn't strictly validate RFC 4180 (e.g., quotes at field boundaries), but this doesn't affect the provided test suite.

Correct implementation passing all test cases with comprehensive RFC 4180 support; state-machine approach cleanly handles quotes, escapes, embedded newlines, custom delimiters, and edge cases; well-structured readable code with helpful comments. Falls slightly short of publication-quality due to surface-level insight and minimal edge-case documentation.

Exceptional solution with correct handling of all RFC 4180 features (quoted fields, escaped quotes, embedded newlines, custom delimiters). Clean state-machine implementation passes all tests. Clear explanation and educational insights. Minor improvements possible with function docstring and inline comments.
Correct and complete async retry implementation with clear explanation; unreachable code at end is minor style issue.

Complete, correct implementation that precisely follows the specification; exponential backoff formula matches requirements exactly; all test cases pass; proper async/await and error handling; includes helpful educational commentary.

Exceptional implementation: correct exponential backoff formula, proper jitter application, correct retry logic with status-code filtering, and all test cases verified to pass. Code is clean, well-commented, properly async/awaits, and demonstrates full understanding of the requirements.

Correct and complete consistent hash ring implementation with O(log n) binary search for get_node. All test cases should pass. Minor deduction: add_node/remove_node use O(n) list operations (bisect.insort and list.remove) instead of more efficient data structures like a balanced tree.

Flawless implementation with correct bisect_left logic for O(log n) clockwise node lookup, proper idempotency via set tracking, correct wrapping behavior, and all test cases should pass including non-trivial distribution uniformity.

Implementation is correct, complete, and efficient. Uses bisect for O(log n) lookups, properly handles idempotency, ring wrapping, and should pass all tests including distribution uniformity. Code is clear with good design decisions explained in the insight.

Excellent comprehensive analysis with perfect time/space complexity reasoning, but two edge case outputs are incorrect: negative numbers case produces [(0, 2)] not [(-5, 0), (0, 1)], and multiple solutions case produces [(2, 3), (0, 4)] not [(0, 2), (3, 4)].

Exceptional analysis covering all required dimensions with accurate complexity claims, detailed identification of the critical duplicate-handling bug with execution traces, comprehensive edge-case coverage, algorithm comparisons, and proposed fixes. Minor redundancy in explanations but overall professional presentation with strong justification throughout.

Exceptional submission—all complexity claims are mathematically correct, all five required analysis points thoroughly addressed, critical bug elegantly identified with concrete traces and fixes, brute-force comparison practical and accurate, structure professional with tables and code examples.

Exceptional implementation. Correct Pratt parser with all binding powers, associativity rules, and error cases handled properly. Handles floats (.5, 1., etc.), unary minus precedence (-2**2 = -4), right-associative **, and all edge cases. Code is well-organized, clearly explained, and all test cases pass.

Correct and complete Pratt parser with sound logic: tokenization handles all numeric formats, nud/led properly implement operator precedence and associativity, unary minus correctly calls expression(2) to allow ** (bp=3) to bind tighter, all error cases covered, and all test cases pass. Minor deduction only for lack of inline code comments.
